threats.title: Critical Math Logic Bug in Vesting Transfer Allows Complete Contract Drain
threats.hypothesis_description: The transferVesting function recalculates the grantor's release rate incorrectly by dividing the total amount by the total number of steps, ignoring already claimed steps. This inflates the release rate, allowing the grantor to claim more tokens than their remaining entitlement, draining the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct violation of the vesting conservation invariant. An attacker can exploit this on-chain without any special privileges to steal tokens belonging to other beneficiaries.
preconditions: Grantor has claimed tokens in previous steps (stepsClaimed > 0)
Grantor transfers a portion of their remaining vesting allocation

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Recalculation of release rate uses total steps instead of remaining steps
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after a partial claim

operational_questions: Can the inflated release rate be used to drain the entire contract balance?
Does the final step revert due to underflow, masking the intermediate theft?
validation_requirements: Simulate a partial claim followed by a transfer and subsequent claims
Verify the contract balance after exploitation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker receives a vesting allocation and waits for several steps to pass
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker transfers a small portion of their remaining allocation to another address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker's release rate is inflated; attacker claims excess tokens in subsequent steps, draining the contract

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of funds for other beneficiaries, protocol insolvency
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen tokens cannot be recovered without a hard fork or admin intervention if tokens are moved

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic understanding of smart contract interactions
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires waiting for vesting steps to elapse

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpectedly high claim amounts compared to original allocation
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed claims by legitimate users due to insufficient contract balance
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Frequent small transfers of vesting allocations followed by claims

threats.title: Complete Bypass of Private Sale Whitelist via Unprotected Function
threats.hypothesis_description: The whitelistAddress function in the SecondSwap_Whitelist contract is external and lacks any access control modifiers. Any user can call this function to add their own address to the whitelist.
threats.investigation_rationale: This completely defeats the purpose of private listings, allowing unauthorized users to purchase allocations intended for specific buyers.
preconditions: totalWhitelist < maxWhitelist
userSettings[msg.sender] == false

initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a private listing is active

operational_questions: Was this intended to be a self-service allowlist, or is it a missing onlyOwner modifier?
Can an attacker front-run legitimate buyers to steal private allocations?
validation_requirements: Test calling whitelistAddress from an unprivileged account
Verify the ability to purchase from a private listing after self-whitelisting

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies a lucrative private listing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls whitelistAddress() on the associated whitelist contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker calls spotPurchase() on the marketplace to buy the private allocation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Private sales can be hijacked by MEV bots or malicious actors
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires deploying a new whitelist contract and migrating listings

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Rapid whitelisting and purchasing by unknown addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Legitimate buyers unable to purchase due to sold-out listings
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: MEV bot activity around private listings

threats.title: Vesting Schedule Corruption via Pooled VestingManager State
threats.hypothesis_description: The VestingManager pools all listed vestings into its own address. When buyers purchase tokens, they inherit the stepsClaimed value of the VestingManager, which is contaminated by previous sellers. This allows buyers to claim tokens earlier or later than intended.
threats.investigation_rationale: This state contamination breaks the core vesting schedule logic for secondary market purchasers, leading to unpredictable and exploitable claim timings.
preconditions: Multiple sellers list vestings with different stepsClaimed values
Buyers purchase these pooled allocations

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: VestingManager acts as an intermediary without isolating individual vesting schedules

operational_questions: How exactly does the VestingManager's stepsClaimed value evolve over multiple listings?
Can a buyer intentionally purchase from a specific pool state to accelerate their vesting?
validation_requirements: Simulate multiple listings from sellers at different vesting stages
Verify the inherited stepsClaimed for subsequent buyers

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker monitors the VestingManager's stepsClaimed state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: When the state is favorable (e.g., high stepsClaimed), attacker purchases a newly listed allocation from an early-stage seller
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker inherits the high stepsClaimed and can immediately claim a large portion of the tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Vesting schedules are bypassed, leading to premature token unlocks
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires complex state migration to fix corrupted schedules

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - requires monitoring state and timing purchases
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Depends on the sequence of legitimate user actions

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Buyers claiming large amounts immediately after purchase
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Inconsistent claimable amounts for buyers of the same vesting plan
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Strategic purchasing based on VestingManager state

threats.title: Uninitialized Implementation Contracts Vulnerable to Takeover
threats.hypothesis_description: The implementation contracts for SecondSwap_Marketplace, SecondSwap_VestingManager, and SecondSwap_VestingDeployer do not disable initializers in their constructors. An attacker can call initialize() directly on the implementation contracts to gain admin control over them.
threats.investigation_rationale: While this does not directly compromise the proxy's state, taking over the implementation contract is a severe operational risk that can lead to phishing, reputational damage, or potential delegatecall exploits depending on the exact proxy architecture.
preconditions: Implementation contracts are deployed
initialize() has not been called on the implementation contracts directly

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing _disableInitializers() in constructors of upgradeable contracts
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after deployment until initialized by an attacker

operational_questions: Can the implementation contract be manipulated to affect the proxy?
Are there any selfdestruct or delegatecall functions in the implementation?
validation_requirements: Check deployment scripts to see if implementation contracts are initialized
Attempt to call initialize() on the implementation contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies the implementation contract addresses from the proxy deployments
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls initialize() on the implementation contracts, setting themselves as admin
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker uses admin privileges on the implementation to execute malicious actions or social engineering

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full control over the implementation contract state
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires upgrading to a new implementation contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Initialization transactions directed at the implementation contracts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected admin addresses in the implementation state

threats.title: Impossible Penalty Fee Configuration Prevents Early Unlisting
threats.hypothesis_description: The penaltyFee is initialized to 10 ether in the SecondSwap_MarketplaceSetting constructor. If the payment token is USDT (which typically has 6 decimals), this equates to 10 trillion USDT, making it impossible for users to pay the penalty fee to unlist their tokens early.
threats.investigation_rationale: This configuration error effectively locks users' tokens in the marketplace until the minListingDuration expires, violating the intended operational workflow for early unlisting.
deployment_assumptions: USDT is used as the penalty fee token and has 6 decimals
preconditions: Marketplace uses USDT for penalty fees
User attempts to unlist before minListingDuration expires

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hardcoded ether value used for an ERC20 token with potentially different decimals
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin has not updated the penalty fee to a reasonable value
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous until admin updates the fee

operational_questions: What is the actual decimal count of the deployed USDT token?
Has the admin updated the penalty fee post-deployment?
validation_requirements: Check the deployed USDT token decimals
Attempt an early unlisting to verify the revert due to insufficient balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User lists tokens on the marketplace
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User attempts to unlist the tokens before the minimum duration expires
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts because the user does not have 10 trillion USDT to pay the penalty

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users cannot cancel their listings, leading to frustration and potential financial loss if market conditions change
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - admin can update the penalty fee

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None - this is a systemic DoS

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed unlistVesting transactions with insufficient balance errors
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: No early unlistings occurring on the platform

threats.title: Referral Rewards Calculated But Never Distributed
threats.hypothesis_description: The _handleTransfers function calculates a referralFeeCost but never actually transfers these tokens to the referrer. Instead, the entire fee amount is sent to the feeCollector. The protocol emits events suggesting the referrer was paid, but no funds are moved.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the protocol's incentive mechanism and results in the feeCollector receiving funds that were intended for referrers, leading to broken trust and incorrect accounting.
preconditions: A purchase is made with a valid referral address
The listing is not private (whitelist == address(0))

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: referralFeeCost is calculated but not used in any transfer call

operational_questions: Is there an off-chain mechanism intended to distribute these fees?
Are referrers aware they are not receiving on-chain payments?
validation_requirements: Execute a purchase with a referral address
Verify the token balances of the referrer and feeCollector

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User acts as a referrer and brings buyers to the platform
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Buyers execute purchases using the referral address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Referrer checks their balance and finds no rewards have been distributed

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Failure of the referral incentive program, reputational damage
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires contract upgrade and manual distribution of owed fees

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Purchased events with non-zero referralReward but no corresponding token transfer to the referral address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: feeCollector accumulating more fees than expected

threats.title: Division by Zero Prevents Transfer of Fully Vested Allocations
threats.hypothesis_description: In the _createVesting function, if a grantor transfers their remaining unclaimed tokens to a new beneficiary after all vesting steps have elapsed (numOfSteps == _stepsClaimed), the releaseRate calculation divides by zero, causing the transaction to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: This prevents users from transferring fully vested but unclaimed tokens to new addresses, forcing them to claim the tokens first, which may not be desired for tax or operational reasons.
preconditions: Grantor has not claimed all tokens
All vesting steps have elapsed (stepsClaimed == numOfSteps)
Grantor attempts to transfer tokens to a new beneficiary

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing check for division by zero when calculating release rate for new beneficiaries
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After the vesting period has fully elapsed

operational_questions: Can users bypass this by transferring to an existing beneficiary instead of a new one?
Does this affect the VestingManager's ability to handle late-stage listings?
validation_requirements: Simulate a transfer to a new address after all steps have passed
Verify the transaction reverts with a division by zero error

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User waits until the vesting schedule is complete
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User attempts to transfer their unclaimed tokens to a new wallet address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts, locking the tokens in the original address until claimed

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users cannot transfer fully vested allocations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - users can simply claim the tokens instead of transferring

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed transferVesting transactions with division by zero errors

threats.title: Unbounded Discount Percentage Causes Permanent Listing DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The listVesting function does not validate the upper bound of discountPct. If a seller sets a discountPct greater than or equal to BASE (10000), the _getDiscountedPrice function will revert due to an underflow, making the listing permanently unpurchasable.
threats.investigation_rationale: This allows sellers to accidentally or intentionally create broken listings that pollute the marketplace and trap their own tokens until they pay the penalty fee to unlist.
preconditions: Seller creates a listing with discountType FIX or LINEAR
discountPct is set >= 10000

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing upper bound validation for discount percentage

operational_questions: Can this be used to grief the marketplace UI by creating unfillable listings?
Are sellers aware they will lose the penalty fee if they make this mistake?
validation_requirements: Create a listing with discountPct = 10001
Attempt to purchase from the listing and verify the revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker creates multiple listings with extreme discount percentages
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Listings populate the marketplace UI, appearing highly attractive
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Buyers attempt to purchase but transactions consistently revert, wasting gas and degrading user experience

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Marketplace pollution, wasted gas for buyers, trapped tokens for sellers
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - sellers can unlist (paying penalty) or admin can unlist

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed spotPurchase transactions with underflow errors

threats.title: Reentrancy in spotPurchase Allows Multiple Purchases of Same Listing
threats.hypothesis_description: The spotPurchase function calls _handleTransfers, which makes external token transfers before updating the listing balance. If the payment token supports callbacks (e.g., ERC777), an attacker can reenter spotPurchase and buy the same listing multiple times before the balance is decremented.
threats.investigation_rationale: While Solidity 0.8 provides overflow protection, careful crafting of purchase amounts could allow multiple reentrant purchases to succeed before the outermost call reverts, draining pooled vesting allocations from the VestingManager.
deployment_assumptions: Admin adds a payment token that supports callbacks (e.g., ERC777)
preconditions: Marketplace supports a payment token with callback functionality
Attacker uses a malicious contract to execute the purchase

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State updates occur after external token transfers (violation of Checks-Effects-Interactions)
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must whitelist a vulnerable token
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While a vulnerable token is supported

operational_questions: Does the protocol intend to support ERC777 or similar tokens?
Can the underflow revert be bypassed by ensuring the final balance exactly hits zero?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock ERC777 token and add it as a supported currency
Execute a reentrancy attack during the transfer hook

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker deploys a malicious contract that implements the token callback hook
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker initiates a spotPurchase via the malicious contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During the token transfer, the hook is triggered, and the attacker reenters spotPurchase
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker purchases the listing multiple times, extracting excess vesting allocations

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of vesting allocations belonging to other users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen allocations are difficult to recover

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - requires custom smart contract deployment
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: High - requires understanding of reentrancy and token hooks

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple spotPurchase events in the same transaction for the same listing
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Use of smart contracts to execute purchases

threats.title: Token Issuer Can Arbitrarily Confiscate User Vesting Allocations
threats.hypothesis_description: The transferVesting function allows the tokenIssuer to specify any address as the grantor. A malicious or compromised token issuer can unilaterally transfer vested tokens from any user's address to their own, confiscating assets purchased on the marketplace.
threats.investigation_rationale: This represents a severe centralization risk and trust boundary violation. While token issuers are semi-trusted, giving them the power to rug-pull individual users undermines the security of the entire secondary market.
preconditions: Attacker is the tokenIssuer or has compromised the tokenIssuer's private key

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: transferVesting does not require the grantor's approval or signature

operational_questions: Is this arbitrary transfer capability an intended feature for compliance/recovery, or a security flaw?
Are token issuers required to use multisig wallets to mitigate key compromise risks?
validation_requirements: Execute transferVesting as the tokenIssuer specifying a different user as the grantor
Verify the tokens are successfully moved without the grantor's consent

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Token issuer's private key is compromised
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls transferVesting on high-value user accounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker transfers the vesting allocations to their own address and claims the tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Massive loss of user funds, complete loss of protocol trust
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires hard fork or legal action

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden movement of large vesting allocations to a single address

threats.title: Marketplace Freeze Permanently Locks Listed Vesting Allocations
threats.hypothesis_description: The unlistVesting function uses the isFreeze modifier. If the admin freezes the marketplace during an emergency, users cannot unlist their tokens to recover them. Even the admin cannot unlist tokens on behalf of users because the modifier applies to all callers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Emergency pause mechanisms should typically allow users to withdraw their funds. By blocking unlisting, the protocol traps user assets during critical operational incidents.
preconditions: Admin sets isMarketplaceFreeze to true
Users have active listings on the marketplace

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Withdrawal/unlisting function is blocked by the emergency pause modifier
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to freeze the marketplace

operational_questions: Was it intended to block unlisting during a freeze, or is this an oversight?
How can users recover their assets if the marketplace is permanently frozen?
validation_requirements: Freeze the marketplace as admin
Attempt to call unlistVesting as a seller and as the admin
Verify both attempts revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin freezes the marketplace due to a suspected vulnerability
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users attempt to withdraw their listed tokens to secure them
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transactions revert, leaving the tokens trapped in the potentially vulnerable VestingManager

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users cannot access their funds during emergencies
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - admin must unfreeze the marketplace to allow withdrawals

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed unlistVesting transactions during a freeze period
